title: Numerical Simulation of Dharali Cloudburst August 2025 Based on WRF Model
authors: Shweta Singh, Meenal Jain, Vivek Kumar Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The Himalayan region has been experiencing increasing intense rainfall, which pose significant challenges to the socio-economic development of India. In recent years, the frequent and severity of rainfall induced disasters, such as, landslides, flash floods and infrastructure damage have escalated drawing considerable attention from the scientific comment. As a result, understanding the underlying drivers and impact of extreme precipitation has drawn considerable attention of research community. This study aims to numerically simulate this extreme weather event to reconstruct its dynamics and assess the predictive capability of Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model. Further, model was configured with high- resolution nested domains to accurately represent the complex terrain of the region. The simulation successfully reflected the intense and highly localized precipitation over the epicenter. The observational findings after simulation was validated against MOSDAC satellite data, which underlines a strong agreement in the timing and intensity of the peak rainfall. These findings underscore that WRF model has high potential to improve existing early warning systems and enhance preparedness for similar high-impact weather events in complex mountainous regions.

title: Simulation Study on FBG Spectrum Peak Detection and Anomaly Detection Based on Gradient Boosted Regression Trees
authors: Zijing Zhou
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors offer advantages such as resistance to electromagnetic interference, high-temperature tolerance, and compact size, making them widely used in industrial monitoring. However, traditional peak detection methods suffer from limited accuracy in complex environments and lack sufficient multi-sensor fusion and anomaly detection capabilities. We propose an intelligent peak detection method based on gradient-boosted regression trees. By constructing a simulation model that incorporates spectral asymmetry, side-lobe effects, and baseline distortion, we achieve the learning and compensation of physical artifacts in FBG spectra. Experimental results show that in single-sensor mode, the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is 0.002694 nm, representing a 25.37% improvement over the Savitzky-Golay method, with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.999903; in a 4-channel network, the average MAE is 0.0404 nm with a standard deviation of 0.0080 nm; anomaly detection based on Random Forest achieves an accuracy of 97.50% across multiple scenarios, validating the method’s precision and robustness.

title: Time and Event-Triggered Fault Detection for the Networked Control System Under DoS Attacks
authors: Wendong Gai, Wenyue Li, Gang Jing, Jing Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: The challenge of fault detection (FD) within networked control system (NCS) experiencing Denial-of-Service (DoS) intrusions is investigated in this paper. A discrete-time system model with variable sampling intervals is established. An observer-based fault detection filter is designed to achieve complete decoupling between event-triggered transmission errors and the residual signal. A time and accumulated-error-eventtriggering mechanism is proposed to improve communication efficiency and ensure reliable monitoring performance. Compared with conventional methods, the proposed scheme reduces communication transmission times by 10.3% and shortens fault detection delay by 3 seconds under DoS attacks. It effectively suppresses the impact of DoS attacks on FD performance. And it provides a practical and robust solution for secure state monitoring of NCS and industrial intelligent platforms.

title: Latent Preference Inference and Expert–Crowd Aggregation in Sequential Elimination
authors: Bokang Han, Feixiang Zhao, Hui Chen, Weiqi Xiao, Jiayu Huang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Many real-world selection systems combine expert evaluation with public preference, yet the latter is often only partially observable. This study investigates how latent crowd preference can be inferred from observable scores and elimination outcomes in a sequential elimination setting. We propose a unified framework that reconstructs hidden support dynamics through a Bayesian state-space formulation, compares alternative aggregation rules through counterfactual analysis, and designs an uncertainty-aware weighting mechanism for expert–crowd decision fusion. The framework is motivated by a dataset spanning 34 seasons of a televised competition in which judge scores are public while audience votes remain hidden. Results show that latent support can be recovered with quantified uncertainty, aggregation rules can materially alter elimination outcomes, and adaptive weighting improves the stability–sensitivity trade-off over fixed schemes. The proposed perspective generalizes beyond entertainment voting and is applicable to hybrid decision systems in ranking, recommendation, and social choice scenarios where preferences are hidden but outcomes are observable.

title: Explainability-Driven Insights into LSTM-Based Sentiment Classification Across Domains
authors: Minal Dhankar, Neha Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Sentiment analysis, which is a foundation of natural language processing, allows for retrieving the emotional overtones of textual data, but the complexity of the deep learning models, like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, leaves much to be desired in regard to their reliability and applicability to real-life scenarios. This paper examines the interpretability of LSTM-based sentiment analysis through the application of existing explainability procedures, such as attention visualisation and feature attribution techniques, to analyze the model decision-making process applied to various datasets that include social media posts and product reviews. By way of systematic analysis, able to unravel the nature of prioritization of contextual dependencies and lexical cues by LSTMs in making positive, negative, and neutral judgments, and also highlight deficiencies in attempts to handle syntactic ambiguities and domain shifts that may result in misclassifications. The results demonstrate how the model is based on sequential patterns to understand subtleties, and potential ways to improve it without modifying architectures. Finally, this paper highlights the need to incorporate the idea of explainability into LSTM applications to promote the development of increased reliability, ethical practice, and interdisciplinary progress in affective computing.

title: Stability-Aware and Phase-Aware Identification of Hallucination-Associated Neurons in LLMs
authors: Zhenhao Xu, Chunhui Yang, Lei Zhao, Yan Gao
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Recent work suggests that a sparse subset of neurons can predict hallucination in large language models, and that such predictors remain informative when transferred from instruction-tuned models to their base counterparts. However, predictive transfer does not by itself establish neuron-level mechanism sharing. A sparse probe may remain effective even when its selected support is unstable, phase-specific, or replaceable by correlated alternatives. This makes one-shot sparse selection too weak a basis for mechanistic claims. We revises hallucination-neuron identification under stricter criteria. A stability-aware and phase-aware framework is proposed to test whether selected neurons are consistently recovered under bootstrap resampling and whether their roles are shared across base and chat models or remain phase-local. Rather than outputting a single flat neuron set, the method decomposes stable neurons into shared, chat-specific, and base-specific subsets. Experiments on Mistral-Small-3.1-24B, Gemma-3-4B, and Llama-3.1-8B over TriviaQA and NQ-Open show that detection performance is retained under the stricter protocol, while only part of the stable support is genuinely shared across phases. These findings narrow the interpretation of cross-phase transfer from mechanism continuity to signal continuity.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Dynamic-Temperature Distillation for Efficient Biomedical Sentence Classification
authors: Jiaxu Dao, Zhuoying Li, Zhiyu Wei, Bing Li, Yujia Cao, Haitao Zhu, Weida He, Yuecheng Yin
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Domain-specific pretrained language models (PLMs) have set new benchmarks in biomedical text mining, yet their immense computational demands hinder deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings. Knowledge distillation (KD) offers a viable compression path, but the optimal integration of diverse distillation signals for specialized biomedical tasks remains underexplored. This paper proposes an Adaptive Multi-Objective Knowledge Distillation framework designed to transfer task-specific expertise from PubMedBERT to a lightweight MiniLM for GAD sentence classification. Our framework features an entropy-guided dynamic-temperature mechanism that adjustably softens teacher logits based on predictive uncertainty, complemented by multi-level feature alignment and relational knowledge transfer. Systematic experiments demonstrate that the distilled student significantly outperforms the non-distilled baseline, achieving an Accuracy of 77.22, F1-score of 80.41, and ROC-AUC of 83.41. Detailed ablation and parameter studies reveal that while logit-level response matching drives primary performance gains, feature-level alignment acts as a sensitive regularizer that requires precise weighting to avoid over-constraining the student’s representational capacity. Our findings provide a practical blueprint for balancing supervised learning with multi-objective teacher guidance in high-precision biomedical NLP applications.

title: An Efficient Cross-Frequency Feature Aggregation Network for Single Image Super-Resolution
authors: Xin Xin, Ruirui Ji, Muyang Tian, Yikun Li, Tong Jia, Chenxi Bai
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) is a fundamental task in computer vision. Existing CNN-based or Transformer-based methods exhibit frequency-domain bias, often focusing on either local high-frequency or global low-frequency information, which limits cross-frequency feature collaboration and reconstruction performance. To address this, this paper proposes FREHMNet, an efficient SISR model with cross-frequency feature aggregation. A dual-branch parallel structure is designed, where the high-frequency aware enhancement branch (FAEB) captures local details and the fourier modulation global branch (FMGB) models global semantics, with the dynamic mixed fusion block (DMFB) enabling adaptive aggregation of cross-frequency features. A lightweight, fast parameter-free attention Block (FAB) is also introduced to strengthen key features and suppress redundancy. Experiments on five public benchmark datasets show that FREHMNet achieves high-quality reconstruction with low parameter count; compared with ×4 SwinIR-light, FREHMNet+ improves average performance by 0.22dB, and reduces FLOPs to only 38%.

title: A Review of the Development of Key Technologies of Embodied Intelligence in the Field of Workplace Safety and Emergency Response
authors: Ge Jin, Jin Pan, Jiaxi Li, Yuchao Gao, Donghan Lv
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: Embodied intelligence provides a new generation of autonomous robot solutions for high-risk industrial scenarios and sudden disaster response by embedding perception, decisionmaking and action into the closed loop of physical environment interaction. In the field of workplace safety and emergency response, terrain complexity, emergency response urgency, human-robot collaboration safety requirements, and the unique and unpredictable characteristics of accidents have become the application problems of embodied intelligence. We focus on how cutting-edge technologies such as World Models, Vision-Language-Action models, and Embodied Foundation Models can improve the autonomy, robustness, and semantic understanding capabilities of robots in unstructured dynamic and uncertain environments. At the same time, the system has sorted out the key technological progress, typical application cases and system architecture of embodied intelligence in the field of workplace safety (including high-risk environment inspection, operation, and human-machine collaboration) and emergency response (including post-disaster search and rescue, hazardous material disposal, and extreme environment operations) since 2020. This paper further discusses the core challenges in perception in extreme environments, dataset, and standard evaluation system, and looks forward to the future research direction for trusted and deployable embodied intelligent systems. This work provides a comprehensive technical reference for both academia and industry, and holds significant value for advancing technological innovation and industrial deployment of embodied intelligence in workplace safety and emergency response.

title: Research on Safe Path Planning for UAV in Unknown Dynamic Environments
authors: Rong Li, Tianfang Xu, Hongru Wang, Mykola Kaliuzhnyi
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: To address the challenges of poor path planning safety, slow convergence, and sparse rewards faced by unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) during high-risk reconnaissance missions, this paper proposes a Fuzzy-Guided Safe Soft Actor-Critic (FG- Safe-SAC) algorithm. This method models path planning as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) and innovatively integrates expert experience to design a Fuzzy Risk Evaluator (FRE). By dynamically evaluating obstacle proximity, heading deviation, and boundary hazard levels in real-time, the algorithm outputs environmental risk coefficients. These coefficients adaptively adjust safety thresholds within the Lagrange dual optimization, achieving an optimal balance between mission gains and violation costs. Simulation experiments demonstrate that under complex training scenarios featuring dynamic enemy UAV and static mountain peaks, FG-Safe-SAC achieves approximately 30% faster convergence than standard SAC, DDPG, and TD3. In Monte Carlo comparison tests with identical obstacle counts, the algorithm achieved a task completion rate of 91.00% with a violation rate of only 12.00%, significantly outperforming DDPG and standard SAC. This algorithm demonstrates exceptional environmental adaptability and robustness, offering an efficient decision-making solution for autonomous and safe navigation of UAVs in unknown dynamic environments.

title: Adaptability Over Accuracy: Strengthening Intrusion Detection Systems for Zero-Day Attacks
authors: Lokku Praveen, Deepika Vatsa, Peddiniti Lalithaditya, Madhuri Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The rapid complexity increase in advanced networking around the world, accelerated its adoption to artificial intelligence and machine learning strategies in intrusion detection systems(IDS). Most recent research works are predominantly focused on benchmark accuracy, however this narrow focus fails to capture model adaptability, and limits its development in production level, particularly under the zeroday attacks. Basically, these zero day attacks are defined as novel and absence of prior signatures to us, these threats pose very critical challenge as they pass traditional detection systems so easily. Our approach in this paper highlights an evaluation framework for addressing the limitation of assessing an intrusion detection system solely over accuracy metrics which are derived from the existing datasets, while mainly concentrating on system adaptability and its resilience. In our study, random forest model reached 99.87% accuracy and 1DCNN model reached 97.54 % accuracy with the same datasets, but when we tested the same models under zero day attack scenario, both the model's performance has dropped by 63.66 % and 60.76 %. Our experimental study results point out to how important it is to shift from the traditional benchmark driven performance accuracy metrics, for ensuring that the intrusion detection systems can withstand new and real time attack seniors, which automatically enhances security resilience across modern network architectures

title: An End-to-End AI-Powered Symptom Checker Using Machine Learning, NLP, and Geolocation for Real-Time Doctor Recommendation
authors: Gaurav Bhatt, Jitendra Nath Singh, Deepak Maurya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Medical diagnosis based on self-diagnosis using the internet has posed mammoth threat of misinformation as people initiate treatment late in life and end up with poor health consequences. The disparity between recorded by a user initially and the actual access of verified and relevant medical experts is very large. The current paper introduces a unified system that is developed in order to address that gap. Not only in predicting potential diseases with the help of machine learning, but the system also links the user with the appropriate local specialist in real time. It is a decoupled web application that consists of a web interface in React (Next.js) and a Python (FastAPI) backend. It incorporates three main technologies: (1) a Random Forest classifier, trained on 132 symptoms and 41 diseases to be predicted; (2) the Google Gemini API to natural language understanding (NLU) to convert user complaints into a structured symptom vector and find predicted diseases as one medical specialty; and (3) the Google Maps API to provide a real-time search based on location to find specialists nearby. Experimental evaluation on a dataset of 4,920 patient records demonstrates that the proposed system achieves up to 100 % accuracy with macro-averaged precision, recall, and F1score on the evaluated dataset. The implemented application is easy to use, responding to the natural language query (e.g., I have cough and fever) to a list of local doctors that a user can act on. The project is an effective evidence-based demonstration of a holistic online health tool. With ML prediction, NLP-driven generative <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex>, and geolocation, the system will provide an effective, practical substitute to unguided online self-diagnosis, which is in line with SDG Goal 3 (Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being).

title: Training Privacy Is Not Inference Privacy: Evaluating Embedding Leakage in Private Federated LLM Fine-Tuning
authors: Ruyang Xiao, Jinguo Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Privacy-preserving federated fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) is commonly evaluated through the lens of training-time protection, typically by perturbing gradients or parameter updates with differential privacy. However, this perspective leaves a deployment-stage question underexplored: do the intermediate embeddings exposed by the resulting model remain private at inference time? We revisit this issue through a compact evaluation paper rather than a new defense proposal. Specifically, we formulate a training-vs.-inference privacy gap, define a lightweight protocol for measuring embedding inversion and client-attribute inference risk, and instantiate the protocol in a pilot federated fine-tuning study on two text classification tasks. Our observations show a consistent pattern: baselines that reduce training leakage can still expose semantically rich intermediate representations during deployment, especially when utility remains high. These findings suggest that training privacy and inference privacy should be reported as separate dimensions in privacy-preserving federated LLM systems.

title: Learning Detailed Representations via Parallel Attention and Local Enhancement for Image Super-Resolution
authors: Weifeng Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: In recent years, transformers have shown excellent performance in computer vision, especially in tasks such as image super-resolution. However, the existing methods often fail to make full use of the powerful global context modeling ability of Transformer and the local detail recovery ability of CNN, and often focus on one of the modeling methods, ignoring the complementary characteristics of the two, resulting in limited image reconstruction quality. To bridge the performance gap between global modeling and local detail recovery, we design FISRNet. FISRNet integrates a transformer-based global branch and a CNN-based local branch, which effectively combines long-range dependencies with texture detail recovery, thereby improving the quality of image reconstruction. FISRNet utilizes Gated Local Interaction Block(GLIB) to capture rich local features, while the Channel Decoupled Relative Attention(CDRA) module further enhances inter-channel dependencies through a spatial-to-channel attention mechanism. Experimental results show that FISRNet has superior performance in a variety of advanced image super-resolution methods. On the Urban100 dataset, FISRNet achieves a PSNR of 28.02dB, which is 0.34dB higher than that of SRFormer, verifying its effectiveness in complex texture scenes.

title: Emotional Participation Modeling and System Optimization of College Students Using Generative AI Chat Robot
authors: Ding Huanhuan, Feng Guoyan, Xia Xiaoxian
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: Under the background of the rapid development of generative AI, the emotional interaction between college students and chat robots is becoming more and more frequent, but their emotional participation generally shows a trend of “high opening and low walking”. Focusing on college students, this paper puts forward “emotional participation” as the core observation index, constructs a multimodal emotional dynamic model integrating behavioral, physiological and cognitive data, and introduces the concept of “emotional interaction threshold” to reveal the minimum stimulus intensity and the best feedback opportunity required to trigger deep emotional participation. This paper studies and designs a layered emotional interaction engine, integrates emotional understanding, generation and optimization modules, and combines personalized strategy base and dynamic update mechanism to realize context adaptation and life cycle extension of AI emotional response. The empirical study is based on 412 college students' samples, and multi-modal data collection and mixed analysis are adopted. The results show that the Emotional Engagement Index (EEI) is significantly improved by the high emotional support system, and the sense of cognitive division is reduced, and the emotional interaction threshold model can effectively predict the opportunity of users' deep participation. The research provides a theoretical model and practical path for the emotional computing design of educational AI system, and emphasizes the key role of personalization, multimodal and dynamic optimization in human-computer emotional interaction.

title: Structured Hybrid-Parallel Normal-Equation Solving for Large-Scale LEO Crosslink Orbit Determination
authors: Tao Chen, Julong Wang, Hao Cao, Xinyu Wang, Chunye Gong
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Crosslink orbit determination for large-scale low Earth orbit (LEO) constellations leads to high-dimensional, strongly coupled, and computationally expensive normal equations. To improve the scalability of conventional globally monolithic assembly and generic iterative solution, this paper proposes a structured hybrid-parallel normal-equation solving method. The method combines topology-aware responsibility-domain partitioning, local block assembly with sparsity-pattern caching, and a distributed block Jacobi-PCG solver under MPI+OpenMP. Experiments show that Topology-aware Block Jacobi-PCG (TA-BJPCG) maintains differential-correction convergence behavior consistent with that of the serial implementation and preserves final orbit-determination accuracy. In a 4000-satellite case, structured assembly reduces sparse-pattern construction overhead by more than 83%, block Jacobi preconditioning cuts the first CG iteration count by about 65%, and TA-BJPCG outperforms PETSc bjacobi+ILU from 4 processes onward while achieving a strong-scaling speedup of 16.31 on 64 processes.

title: A 4.19-M Pixels Colloidal Quantum Dot-Based SWIR Image Sensor With High-Linearity Multistep Single-Slope ADCs
authors: Lixiang Han, Yihui Zhang, Hao Li, Junjie Chen, Yingxiang Liang, Yuanbo Zhang, Chengjie Deng, Liang Gao, Jiang Tang, Dongsheng Liu, Ang Hu
abstract: This article presents a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2048\times 2048$</tex-math> </inline-formula> pixels colloidal quantum dot (CQD) short-wave infrared (SWIR) image sensor with multistep single slope analog-to-digital converters (SS ADCs). The quantization of the multistep SS ADC is divided into three parts, including most-significant-bit (MSB) judgment, 4-bit coarse quantization, and 8-bit fine quantization, which contains one redundant bit. Special heterogeneous integration processes have been developed for CQD devices and CMOS readout integrated circuits (ROICs), including front-end and back-end processes. The proposed image sensor was fabricated in SMIC 0.18-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m mixed signal CMOS process with a pixel pitch of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m, a column-level ADC with the area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times 830~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m, and a frame rate of 30 frames/s at 20 MHz system clock. The measured differential nonlinearity (DNL) of the ADC is −0.44/+0.46 least significant bit (LSB) and the integral nonlinearity (INL) is −0.79/+1.1 LSB. A pixel fill factor (FF) of over 81% is achieved by the proposed heterogeneous integration processes.

title: A Novel Physics-Informed Transformer Algorithm (PITA) for Near–Real-Time Synergistic Multi-Parameter Aerosol Retrieval From FY-4B/AGRI Geostationary Satellite
authors: Yingzi Jiao, Tianhe Wang, Sichen Wang, Yidan Si, Lin Chen, Yu Zheng, Fan Yang, Chenglong Zhou, Leiku Yang, Bakhriddin E. Nishonov, Mansur O. Amonov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate retrieval of aerosol properties from geostationary satellites is critical for capturing rapid atmospheric variability, yet remains difficult due to uncertain aerosol-model assumptions, clear-pixel misclassification over bright surfaces, and limited physical interpretability of purely data-driven approaches. We present a Physics-Informed Transformer Algorithm (PITA) for aerosol retrieval that couples radiative transfer (RT) physics with a Transformer architecture to enable near – real-time synergistic retrieval of aerosol optical depth (AOD) at 550 nm and the Ångström exponent (AE; 440–675 nm) from Fengyun-4B (FY-4B) Advanced Geosynchronous Radiation Imager (AGRI) observations. PITA integrates: 1) six regionally optimized seasonal aerosol models derived via deep clustering of 11-year ground-based aerosol observations, reducing the mean absolute error (MAE) of forward-simulated apparent reflectance by 57.6% relative to MODIS Collection 6 models; 2) a critical-reflectance clear-pixel identification scheme to improve screening over bright surfaces; and 3) an RT-constrained Transformer network with RT-derived features, with SHAP attribution indicating these physics features contribute > 70% of model importance. Sample-based ten-fold cross-validation yields AOD performance of R = 0.94 and MAE = 0.07 (76% within the expected error, EE) and AE performance of R = 0.77 and MAE = 0.16 (93% within EE). Compared with the operational FY-4B land aerosol product, PITA improves AOD correlation by 52.5% and increases AE correlation from − 0.12 to 0.73. Sensitivity analyses show negligible bias at low aerosol loading (AOD < 0.23) and weak dependence on particle size. Without external reanalysis inputs, PITA offers a physically interpretable framework for near – real-time, high-accuracy geostationary aerosol monitoring.

title: A Lightweight WiFi-based Human Activity Recognition Method for Unknown Domains
authors: Wanguo Jiao, Liyan Shao, Wei Du, Shuai Ma
abstract: The unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) strategy is widely used to solve cross-domain human activity recognition based on the WiFi channel state information (CSI). However, the UDA strategy requires collecting large unlabeled target domain data in advance, which is highly impractical for real-world deployments. Considering the strict unavailability of target domain data in practical single-transceiver scenarios, a lightweight recognition method based on the domain generalization (DG) strategy is proposed. To extend the spatiotemporal features of the activity samples, the raw amplitude and phase of the CSI data are integrated using the proposed Pseudo-Heatmap imaging algorithm. Based on the multi-source domain generalization strategy, a cross-domain recognition model, DGWiAR, is designed. DGWiAR consists of a data augmentation module, a teacher-student distillation module, a feature alignment module, and a classifier. The model divides the learning of domain-invariant features into two parallel parts: inter-domain and intra-domain. By embedding a deep residual network as the teacher network to guide the lightweight student network, the model can achieve a balance between the intra-domain invariant feature extraction ability of the student network and the computational cost. By using different source domains to imitate the unknown target domain, CORAL is utilized to align the feature distributions between the source and pseudo-target domains to learn inter-domain invariant features. Meanwhile, a constraint based on cosine distance is used to maintain the differences between inter-domain and intra-domain features to prevent the two types of features from crossing during the learning process. Additionally, considering the temporal characteristics of Pseudo-Heatmap images, DGWiAR introduces a data augmentation module based on a custom masking strategy to alleviate overfitting during training and enhance the robustness of the model to noise. Some available data has been used to train the model, then the trained lightweight student network is used to recognize activities from other unknown domains. The experimental results show that the accuracy of DGWiAR achieves 95.5%, 94.4%, 91.3%, and 93.0% across locations, orientations, users, and environments of a single link, respectively, and effectively reduces the complexity simultaneously.

title: Exploring Basic Expression Representation for Compound Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Rongkang Dong, Cuixin Yang, Cong Zhang, Jun Xiao, Kin-Man Lam
abstract: The field of automatic Facial Expression Recognition (FER) has predominantly focused on classifying basic expressions. However, real-world facial expressions are often more complex, involving compound expressions that combine basic expressions within a single facial expression. This work aims to advance Compound Facial Expression Recognition (CFER), a more challenging task in FER. We identify two critical limitations in existing facial expression datasets: the scarcity of compound expression data and the presence of data ambiguity in basic expression datasets, where samples with compound expressions are labeled as basic expressions. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework called Dual Basic data Enhancement Learning (DuBEL). DuBEL analyzes compound facial expressions and divides basic expression data into potential compound and purer basic expressions. By leveraging this distinction, the framework enables models to recognize compound facial expressions through their underlying basic expressions. DuBEL employs a dual enhancement training strategy for basic expression data, comprising two key schemes. The first is a pseudo compound expression training scheme, which relabels and utilizes potential compound expression samples to enrich the learning of compound expressions. The second is a basic expression enhancement training scheme, which strengthens basic expression representation learning by incorporating purer basic expression samples. Experimental results demonstrate that DuBEL effectively identifies compound expression samples within basic expression datasets and enhances the representation of basic expressions. Our method significantly improves CFER performance, achieving state-of-the-art results.

title: Digital Transformation with Metaverse-Based Services: An Exploratory Managerial Framework Using AI-Simulated Stakeholder Dialogues
authors: Abdul Rahman Abdel Razek, Thomas Meiren, Steffen Braun, Katharina Hölzle
abstract: This study aims at bridging the gap between Metaverse theory and practical application by developing a service innovation framework intended to serve as a decision support tool for managers. This study's insights should guide managers in selecting, configuring, and implementing metaverse-based services. Drawing on prior empirical work that identified six fundamental business model patterns in the Metaverse, this study applies AI-augmented, AI-simulated stakeholder dialogues with several personas reflecting stakeholder perspectives to synthesize model-generated insights. The resulting Metaverse services guide, attribute checklist and implementation guidelines provide actionable insights and guidance for development and implementation of metaverse based services. Findings reveal that organizational readiness, integration requirements, cost-benefit considerations, and regulatory compliance are critical success factors shaping adoption strategies trajectories. By linking business model theory with Metaverse based services development and implementation, this study delivers corroborated tools and roadmaps, enabling indication-based decision making for managers navigating the complexities of Metaverse service innovation.

title: Comparative Study of Avalanche and Short-Circuit Reliability in 1.2-kV SiC MOSFETs
authors: Kazuhiro Suzuki, Hiroshi Yano, Noriyuki Iwamuro
abstract: This study evaluates and compares the specific <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {on,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>)–short-circuit withstand time (SCWT) tradeoff characteristics and unclamped inductive switching (UIS) capabilities of state-of-the-art 1.2-kV SiC MOSFETs. The following five commercial devices were evaluated: two asymmetric trench MOSFETs (one new and one conventional), an advanced double-trench MOSFET, and two planar MOSFETs. The new asymmetric trench MOSFET achieved the lowest <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {on,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> due to reduced channel and J-FET resistance, which was enabled by fine cell pitch, higher channel mobility, and J-FET doping. The advanced double-trench MOSFET exhibited insufficient UIS capability and abnormal gate leakage. Its failure analysis and technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations revealed that trench gates placed beneath the gate runner caused electric field concentration at trench corners, leading to oxide stress and leakage. Therefore, removing trench gates from these regions is critical for improving UIS robustness. The <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {on,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and SCWT tradeoff analysis showed that trench MOSFETs outperform planar types. To extract and quantify the constituent components of the <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance in both the linear and saturation operating regions, a comprehensive analytical approach utilizing experimentally obtained current–voltage characteristics was adopted. Under high-current-density conditions, trench devices exhibited increased J-FET resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {J-FET}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) due to deep p+ regions, which suppressed saturation current and enhanced SCWT. This dual behavior (low <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {on,sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in linear regions and suppressed current in saturation regions) was identified as a key design advantage. The authors conclude that fine-pitch trench gates, deep p+ regions, and careful gate layouts are essential for achieving superior performance in SiC MOSFETs.

title: Simultaneous Estimation of Nonlinear Functionals of a Quantum State
authors: Kean Chen, Qisheng Wang, Zhan Yu, Zhicheng Zhang
abstract: We consider a fundamental task in quantum information theory, estimating the values of tr(Oρ), tr(Oρ2), . . . , tr(Oρk) for an observable O and a quantum state ρ. We show that Θ(k) samples of ρ are sufficient and necessary to simultaneously estimate all the k values. This means that estimating all the k values is almost as easy as estimating only one of them, tr(Oρk). As an application, our approach advances the sample complexity of entanglement spectroscopy and the virtual cooling for quantum many-body systems. Moreover, we extend our approach to estimating general functionals by polynomial approximation.

title: Enhancing Weather Forecasting through CrIS and IASI Imagery
authors: Wayne McCullough, Flavio Iturbide-Sanchez, William Line, Jonathan Wrotny, Subhatra Sivam
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Satellite imagery from weather satellites plays a critical role in the operational weather forecasting process. For example, forecasters view animations of water vapor imagery to detect features that are forcing, or may force, the development of weather phenomena, and to assess the performance of NWP models. While the current generation of geostationary and polar orbiting satellite imagers have robust spectral capabilities for a variety of applications, they are lacking in certain areas where sounders are capable of providing new information. At low and mid-latitudes, water vapor imagery from geostationary infrared instruments is plentiful, temporally, and readily used by forecasters. However, at high latitudes, geostationary-based data is not available while polar orbiting infrared imagers, like the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS), lack water vapor bands valuable for weather forecasting applications. To fill the data gap of water vapor imagery in the high latitudes, we utilize data from multiple polar-orbiting hyperspectral infrared sounders to generate unique forecaster-friendly and relevant water vapor imagery. This is achieved by combining observations from the NOAA Cross-track Infrared Sounder (CrIS) and European Organisation for the Exploitation of Meteorological Satellites (EUMETSAT) Infrared Atmospheric Sounder Interferometer (IASI) to create imagery composites capable of providing full geographic coverage of the North and South polar regions. In this manuscript, a range of case studies highlight the practical value of exploiting unique and new CrIS and IASI imagery in real-world applications.

title: MLPerf Power: Addressing the AI Power Wall
authors: Arya Tschand, Sachin Idgunji, Arun Tejusve Raghunath Rajan, David Kanter, Vijay Janapa Reddi
abstract: Power has become the defining constraint for modern machine learning, from milliwatt edge devices to multi-megawatt training clusters. Across this nine-order-of-magnitude span, the field has lacked a consistent way to measure and compare energy efficiency. We present MLPerf Power, a cross-scale energy benchmarking methodology that adapts instrumentation while preserving common measurement principles and reports shared metrics such as samples per joule and tokens per joule. The methodology has produced 1,841 reproducible submissions from 60 systems. The corpus shows that easy efficiency gains are plateauing, that energy scales nonlinearly with accelerator count, and that low-precision innovation has substantially reduced the cost of high-accuracy inference. Extending to LLM inference, we show that sparse mixture-of-experts models pay a memory-bandwidth penalty that contradicts training intuitions. The publicly accessible corpus already informs industry roadmaps, supports infrastructure planning, and provides ground-truth telemetry for energy-aware optimization and verified carbon accounting in AI systems.

title: Novel Resonance Degree Discrimination Algorithm for Enhanced Systematic Compact Neuro-TF Parametric Modeling of Microwave Components
authors: Peng Zhang, Jinyi Liu, Feng Feng, Wei Liu, Kaixue Ma, Qi-Jun Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a novel resonance degree discrimination algorithm for the systematic compact embedded neuro-transfer function (SC-TF) parametric modeling method. As the variation range of geometrical parameters expands, the electromagnetic (EM) responses of microwave components exhibit stronger fluctuations, which lead to the emergence of abnormal pole–residue pairs and inconsistent distributions of high- and low-resonance sub-TFs under different geometrical parameters. To address these issues, a three-step strategy is introduced, which incorporates preliminary pole–residue classification, sub-TF scaling, and adaptive pole–residue tracking. The proposed algorithm effectively improves the modeling accuracy and applicability range of the SC-TF method. The effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is verified through two microwave filter examples.

title: Design Space Exploration of RISC-V Vector Extension targeting Embedded Processors
authors: Willian Analdo Nunes, Antônio Vinicius Corrêa dos Santos, Lucas Damo, Fernando Gehm Moraes
abstract: The RISC-V Vector Extension (RVV) offers flexibility and scalability for the growing demand for data-parallel workloads in embedded systems. However, RVV configurability introduces a large design space, the implications of which for resource-constrained processors remain insufficiently quantified. Understanding how parameters such as vector length (VLEN), maximum element size (ELEN), lane count, LMUL, and SEW affect system-level metrics enables efficient acceleration while preserving software portability and reducing verification effort. This work explores the RVV design space for embedded processors using a Zve32x subset and evaluates the impact of architectural parameters on performance, area, power, and energy efficiency. Annotated post-synthesis simulations across representative benchmarks enable quantitative trade-off analysis over multiple RVV configurations. Results show that larger VLENs and aggressive LMUL settings can yield substantial speedups but often increase area and power, whereas moderate configurations provide improved energy efficiency while meeting performance targets. The paper derives practical design guidelines for selecting RVV parameters in power- and areaconstrained embedded systems. The RISC-V core used in this work is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/gaph-pucrs/RS5</uri>.

title: A Wormlike Robot for Ferromagnetic Surface Inspection: A Novel System Inspired by Caterpillars
authors: Parisa Parhami, Mohammad Hossein Salehpour, Hamed Hamzeh, Rezvan Nasiri, Hadi Moradi
abstract: Industrial inspection is a crucial task for ensuring the longterm maintenance of infrastructures with ferromagnetic surfaces, such as power plants, wind turbines, and chemical facilities. These environments are often unsafe for human access, motivating the use of climbing robots to perform inspection tasks reliably and repeatedly; nevertheless, the existing robots often have limited maneuverability, reduced stability during transitions, and high energy consumption. In this article, we present a novel robotic system inspired by the body morphology of caterpillars. The design consists of three modules connected via a spring joint mechanism and equipped with a passive magnetic adhesion system. The design’s maneuverability, stability, energy efficiency, and falling robustness are studied analytically and experimentally. Experimental results confirmed the robot’s ability to perform internal/external corner transitions, thin surface transitions, pipe circling, and surface turning in real-world environments, including rusty, coated, and uneven surfaces. Furthermore, we found that compared with simple locomotion, the designed wormlike locomotion yielded an average 10.2% increase in forward velocity and 6.7% reduction in the cost of transportation (COT). These findings suggest that the proposed design offers a practical and versatile approach for inspecting ferromagnetic surfaces.

title: Filtering Multibeam Antenna Arrays Based on Substrate Integrated Waveguide Filtering Crossovers: A Proof of Concept
authors: Shuhao Shen, Kang Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A novel methodology for implementing filtering multibeam antenna arrays (MAAs) based on substrate integrated waveguide (SIW) filtering crossovers (FCRs) is proposed and validated through comprehensive analysis, design, and experiments. Two novel types of topologies for filtering MAAs are proposed based on either a dual-channel FCR with inherent filtering responses or a quad-channel FCR with reconfigured signal routing in the Butler matrix. As a proof of concept, two filtering MAAs based on the two types of topologies are designed and developed using SIW technology. To meet the strict spatial constraints within the Butler matrix, two kinds of highly compact SIW FCRs are developed with hybrid structures combining square and sector resonant cavities, thus significantly reducing the interconnection areas with preceding components. Through employing the quad-channel FCR topology, the footprint is further reduced via sharing more dual-mode cavities. Finally, the proposed two filtering MAAs are fabricated and measured; the results demonstrate stable multibeam radiation patterns and inherent filtering performance. To the best of our knowledge, this work represents the first attempt to implement complete filtering beamforming networks (BFNs) and filtering MAAs by directly incorporating filtering functionality into crossovers. Compared to conventional methods using filtering couplers or filtering phase shifters, the proposed approach offers the advantages of broad applicability, simple structure and methodology, as well as high integration and low loss, which offers a promising solution for next-generation highly integrated and ultracompact multifunctional wireless communication and sensing systems.

title: Hyperspectral Unmixing Under Endmember Variability: A Variational Inference Framework
authors: Yuening Li, Xiao Fu, Junbin Liu, Wing-Kin Ma
abstract: This work proposes a variational inference (VI) framework for hyperspectral unmixing in the presence of endmember variability (HU-EV). An EV-accounted noisy linear mixture model (LMM) is considered, and the presence of outliers is also incorporated into the model. Following the marginalized maximum likelihood (MML) principle, a VI algorithmic structure is designed for probabilistic inference for HU-EV. Specifically, a patch-wise static endmember assumption is employed to exploit slow spatial variations of endmembers and to overcome the ill-posed nature of the HU-EV problem. The design facilitates lightweight, continuous optimization-based updates under a variety of endmember priors. Some of the priors, such as the Beta prior, were previously used under computationally heavy, sampling-based probabilistic HU-EV methods. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is demonstrated through synthetic, semi-real, and real-data experiments.

title: The Asymptotic Capacity of Private Information Retrieval with Secure Storage Under Disjoint Colluding Sets
authors: Haobo Jia, Zhuqing Jia
abstract: In this paper, we consider the problem of private information retrieval with secure storage (SS-PIR) under a setting with disjoint colluding sets, where the user wishes to privately retrieve one out of K independent messages that are securely stored across N servers. The N servers are partitioned into M disjoint colluding sets, i.e., within the m-th group, m ∈ [M], any set of up to Tm colluding servers cannot learn any information about the index of the desired message, and any set of up to Xm colluding servers cannot learn any information about the K messages. The asymptotic capacity is defined as the maximum possible number of q-ary symbols of the desired message that can be retrieved per q-ary downloaded symbol, in the limit as the number of messages K → ∞. We demonstrate that the asymptotic capacity of SS-PIR with disjoint colluding sets is the solution to a linear program parameterized by the server partition, privacy thresholds Tm, and security thresholds Xm. Our achievability scheme introduces a novel pre-decoding strategy built upon cross-subspace alignment (CSA) codes. In this strategy, instead of requiring the user to decode the message from all of the original CSA coded answer symbols, our approach allows certain servers to perform a local pre-decoding and return intermediate results that turn out to be more communication-efficient. This mechanism is the key to minimizing the download cost, allowing our scheme to match the information-theoretic converse and thereby establish the asymptotic capacity of SS-PIR with disjoint colluding sets.

title: Research on Nonlinear Vibration Suppression Optimization of Gear Transmission System Based on Deep Learning Fine-Tuning
authors: Haojie Wen, Xiaoxia Jiang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: The gear transmission system serves as a critical power transmission component in mechanical equipment, with its operational stability directly influencing the overall performance and service life of the machinery. However, under high-speed and heavy-load operating conditions, nonlinear factors such as tooth backlash, time-varying mesh stiffness, and frictional nonlinearity during the gear meshing process can induce complex nonlinear vibrations. These vibrations may lead to reduced transmission accuracy, increased noise levels, and even structural fatigue failure. Conventional vibration suppression techniques often struggle to accommodate the time-varying nature of nonlinear dynamic behaviors under complex operating conditions, exhibiting limitations in control precision and robustness. To address these challenges, this study proposes a deep learning-based fine-tuning approach for optimizing nonlinear vibration suppression in gear transmission systems. A dynamic model incorporating multiple sources of nonlinearities is developed, enabling the quantification of the mapping relationships between key parameters-such as tooth backlash and time-varying mesh stiffness-and the resulting vibration response. A vibration prediction model is developed based on the pre-trained ResNet-18 architecture, with enhanced fitting accuracy for nonlinear vibration characteristics achieved through fine-tuning the parameters of the fully connected layers. Utilizing the predicted vibration responses as feedback, a vibration suppression strategy is formulated using model predictive control (MPC), and the proposed approach is experimentally validated on a gear transmission test rig. Experimental results demonstrate that, within the rotational speed range of 1000–3000 r/min, the proposed method reduces the peak radial vibration acceleration of the gear system by 38.2%–45.7% and the root mean square (RMS) value by 32.5%–40.1%, significantly outperforming conventional PID control. This study presents a novel technical framework for mitigating nonlinear vibrations in complex mechanical systems and holds substantial potential for engineering applications.
